title: Political budget cycles and the civil service: Evidence from highway spending in US states
summary_en: Politicians often spend more in election years (political budget cycles). But is this worse in states where civil-service protections are weaker? This paper uses US state-level highway-spending data to answer. Result: political budget cycles in highway spending are significantly larger in states with weaker civil-service rules. Civil-service protections, in effect, act as a commitment device that limits electoral manipulation of public spending. A clean empirical confirmation of the political-economy argument for bureaucratic independence.
data_used: US state-level highway spending data with political-cycle identification. Cross-state variation in civil-service protections.
policy_relevance: Directly relevant to Hungarian governance-reform debates (BM, central administration). The paper provides empirical support for civil-service protections as a tool limiting electoral manipulation of public spending. Relevant to Hungarian public-procurement reform, MÁSZ (municipal-affairs agency), and EU cohesion-fund oversight. Caveat: US state-level evidence; Hungarian institutional context (unitary rather than federal, different electoral cycles) changes the quantitative implications.
